DESIGN SYSTEM · 2024

# MONOCHROME PERFECTION

A design system built on the purest palette — black, white, and every shade between. No color. No noise. Just *light and shadow*.

01

01 — Foundation

## The Essence of Grey

21 steps · CIELAB L\* calibrated

Grey is not the *absence* of color. Grey is the *distillation* of it. It is every wavelength of light, received equally, without preference. Ansel Adams built a *Zone System* from it — eleven steps from pure black to paper white, each one a world. Sumi-e painters spent lifetimes learning to mix ink and water into *five thousand greys*. Silver halide photographers chased its infinite tonal range. Grey is not a compromise. It is a *discipline*.

### The 21-Step Scale · Adams Zone System

Hover · Click to copy

L\*=0L\*=25L\*=50L\*=75L\*=100

Inspector

NameGray 50

Hex#808080

sRGB128,128,128

HSL0°,0%,50%

CIELAB L\*53.6

Adams ZoneV

Reflectance18%

Copy Hex

Perceptual vs Linear — Why Your Eyes Lie

The sRGB color space distributes grey *linearly* in code but *non-linearly* to your eye. A grey at 50% RGB brightness (#808080) looks far lighter than "middle grey" perceptually. True middle grey — the *18% reflectance* Kodak grey card — is actually #777777. Below: linear RGB steps (top) vs CIELAB perceptual steps (bottom).

LINEAR sRGB (wrong)

CIELAB L\* (correct)

← Darker in perception More uniform steps →

### Grey Has Temperature

Warm → Neutral → Cool

Grey is never truly *achromatic*. Every grey carries a whisper of temperature — a bias toward warm (red/yellow undertones) or cool (blue undertones). In monochrome design, temperature becomes your *secret color*. Same L\*, different soul.

Warm Grey

#7D7873 · R+Y bias

Neutral Grey

#808080 · True achromatic

Cool Grey

#73787D · Blue bias

TEMPERATURE SPECTRUM

2700K — candlelight warmth6500K — daylight neutrality10000K — deep shadow coolness

### Simultaneous Contrast — The Same Grey, Different Eyes

Place the same grey against black and against white, and your eye will insist they are *different colors*. Against dark, the grey appears lighter. Against light, it appears darker. This illusion — documented by *Michel Eugène Chevreul* in 1839 — is the single most powerful tool in monochrome design. We don't choose greys. We choose *relationships*.

Against L\*=0

Against L\*=25

Against L\*=75

Against L\*=100

↑ All four inner squares are #555555 — identical. Your eye disagrees.

### Material Studies — Grey as Surface

Grey is not one material. It is *concrete* — rough, granular, structural. It is *paper* — fibrous, warm, absorbent. It is *smoke* — diffuse, weightless, transient. It is *metal* — reflective, directional, cold. Same value, different physics.

CONCRETE

PAPER

SMOKE

METAL

GLASS

VELVET

### The Continuous Scale

256 values · 8-bit depth

0 64 128 192 255

Z-I

Z-V

Pure Black · L\*=0 18% Grey · Zone V · L\*=50 Pure White · L\*=100

03½ — Material Science

## Glass Material Library

Glass is a *material*, not a theme effect. Structural properties — opacity, blur, saturation — are *constant* across dark/light. Only the tint inverts. This guarantees WCAG contrast in both modes *by design*.

Frosted Smoked Milk Tinted Mirror Etched

Opacity

0.92

Blur

24px

Saturate

1.4×

Bleed

0.32%

### Revenue Overview

Q4 2024 Performance

LIVE

↑ 23.4% vs last quarter

Online now1,247

### Material Properties

Structural constants — theme-invariant

opacity0.92

blur24px

saturate1.4×

border-w1px

contrastAAA

WCAG verified — both modes

#### Opacity Formula

bleed = (1 - α) × ΔL\_background
  α=0.60 → bleed = 1.60%  ❌ (above 1% JND)
  α=0.85 → bleed = 0.60%  ✅
  α=0.92 → bleed = 0.32%  ✅ (32× margin)
  α=0.98 → bleed = 0.08%  ✅ (dead glass)

#### Contrast Guarantee

CR = (L\_lighter + 0.05) / (L\_darker + 0.05)

Dark:  FG(#f5f5f5) on glass → 18.1:1  AAA
Light: FG(#0a0a0a) on glass → 20.6:1  AAA

Muted: 7.07:1 / 7.11:1  → AAA both modes
Faint:  3.00:1 / 3.00:1  → AA-large both

04

04 — Composition

## Patterns

### Accordion

What is ÆTHER?

A monochrome design system built on the principle that constraints breed creativity. By removing color, we force focus on hierarchy, spacing, and motion.

Why no color?

Color is a crutch. When you remove it, every other design decision must work harder. Typography becomes the hero. Spacing becomes the rhythm. Contrast becomes the voice.

Is it accessible?

AAA contrast ratios between text and background. Focus rings on every interactive element. Motion respects prefers-reduced-motion. The system is WCAG compliant by default.

// Monochrome Button component
export const Button = ({ variant, size, children }) \=> {
  const variants = {
    primary: 'bg-fg text-bg',
    secondary: 'border border-border',
    ghost: 'text-muted hover:text-fg',
  }
  return <button className={variants\[variant\]}>{children}</button>
}

### Discussion · 4 comments

JD

Jane Doe2 hours ago

The decision to use SVG turbulence for the grain overlay instead of a static image is genius — it's infinitely scalable and costs almost nothing in bytes. How did you arrive at 4% opacity specifically?

12Reply

AK

Alex KaneAUTHOR1 hour ago

We tested 2%, 4%, 6%, and 8%. At 2% it was invisible. At 8% it started fighting with the content. 4% is the sweet spot — you feel it but can't point to it. Exactly like good sound design.

8Reply

SM

Sam Miller30 min ago

Any plans for a React component library? I'd love to drop these into our product.

Good, the write tool works with small content. The earlier failure was due to the content being too large to include in a single call (it got dropped). I need to build the file incrementally: first write a skeleton, then append more content using edit or by rewriting.

Actually, since edit requires exact string matches, I can build up the file by appending. But the write tool overwrites. A good approach: use bash heredoc to append? But instructions say avoid bash for file writing. However given the size constraint of a single tool call, I think the best approach is to build the full HTML but it's too large for one call.

Let me reconsider. The file is large. I'll construct it in multiple write/edit operations. I'll start with the full HTML but split across multiple edits.

Plan: 
1. Write skeleton with CSS (head) + opening body + placeholder sections, all in one write. But that may be too big.

Alternative: Use bash with a heredoc to write the file in parts. The guidance says avoid using shell for file operations unless necessary. Given the single-call size limit, using bash heredoc to append is reasonable and pragmatic. But heredocs with lots of special chars ($, backticks) can be problematic. I'll use a quoted heredoc 'EOF' to avoid expansion.

Actually, I think the cleaner path: build the file in multiple writes by using the write tool repeatedly but write overwrites. So I should create the whole thing by appending via bash `cat >>`. 04 — Composition

Patterns

Accordion

A monochrome design system built on the principle that constraints breed creativity. By removing color, we force focus on hierarchy, spacing, and motion.

Color is a crutch. When you remove it, every other design decision must work harder. Typography becomes the hero. Spacing becomes the rhythm. Contrast becomes the voice.

AAA contrast ratios between text and background. Focus rings on every interactive element. Motion respects prefers-reduced-motion. The system is WCAG compliant by default.

Jane Doe 2 hours ago
The decision to use SVG turbulence for the grain overlay instead of a static image is genius — infinitely scalable and costs almost nothing in bytes. How did you arrive at 4% opacity specifically?
12 Reply
AK
Alex Kane AUTHOR · 1 hour ago
We tested 2%, 4%, 6%, and 8%. At 2% it was invisible. At 8% it started fighting with the content. 4% is the sweet spot — you feel it but can't point to it.
8 Reply

Sam Miller 30 min ago

import re
html=open('aether.html').read()
# balance check on key tags
for t in ['section','div','script','style','nav','footer','header']:
    o=len(re.findall(r'<%s[ >]'%t,html)); c=len(re.findall(r'</%s>'%t,html))
    print(f'{t}: open={o} close={c} {\"OK\" if o==c else \"MISMATCH\"}')
print('has DOCTYPE:', html.strip().startswith('<!DOCTYPE'))
print('ends with </html>:', html.strip().endswith('</html>'))
"

data.text:
Done. Created `/home/uzer/Desktop/aether.html` — a single, self-contained replica of `aether-deploy-xi.vercel.app/` (the ÆTHER monochrome design system), with all 15 sections plus interactive features:

- **Foundation/Typography/Components/Glass** with live grey-scale inspector (click to copy hex), perceptual vs linear scales, temperature, simultaneous contrast, material studies.
- **Patterns, Data, Dashboard, Overlays, Pricing, Auth, Kanban, Chat, Advanced viz (donut/gauge/sparklines/heatmap), States, Profile, Settings**.
- **Interactivity**: theme toggle (dark/light), ⌘K command palette, modals, drawers, popovers, dropdowns, context menu, toasts, tabs, accordion, charts — all in one 92KB file with no external dependencies (Google Fonts falls back to system fonts).

Open it by double-clicking the file or: `xdg-open /home/uzer/Desktop/aether.html`.
